comment.body: If we are trying to think from first principles about likely eu reaction and the results. What can they actually do and what are they likely to do? The member nations have such different interests. They can’t just drop all the import duties on us goods and have their market flooded with US products hurting EU companies. They also don’t want the war to escalate too much hurting EU consumers. They also can’t look like pushovers. You could say that EUs best course would be the most toothless response they can manage, that looks tough to the public. but they really are stuck because if it’s too weak they may as well not reciprocate and if it’s too strong looking then trump will go so hard in response they will regret it. It really is a tightrope they are walking

comment.body: Seems EU companies with big EU consumer exposure are in big trouble either way here in short term, scenario 1. tarrifs stick and markets tank further they suffer from consumers having less money to spend due to higher prices and consumer worry.  Scenario 2. All us goods come into eu free of tarrifs and they lose lots of market share.

comment.body: EU is not a union, never was. Started with failure to consolidate debt of member countries, which would have been necessary BEFORE creating a common currency. Now the left leaning main countries like France and Germany are trying to take a strong anti-US approach ('Weimar Triangle', including Poland) while others as Greece and Italy are contemplating EU exit. Meloni was the only EU head of state to attend Trump‘s inauguration. Brussels is warmongering as they know EU is facing a sovereign debt crisis. That is why they keep supporting & financing Zelenskij. EU will not negotiate any peace deal nor acknowledge any deal between Trump & Putin.
